responses.strategic_rationale: The levers address core uncertainties in launching a first-stage residential parasomnia research unit by balancing capacity constraints, data quality, participant burden, and funding. Through throughput optimization, privacy-conscious sensing, and a staged data-sharing framework, the plan seeks to maximize valid event capture while preserving safety and regulatory compliance. Diversified recruitment approaches and flexible staffing reduce enrollment risk and improve retention, enabling robust longitudinal phenotyping. A governance and funding mix—pragmatic grant strategy, industry partnerships with clear publication terms, and transparent oversight—creates a credible path toward phase-two expansion and standardized methods for multi-site validation. These levers collectively align with Bonn's long-term video-EEG strength and ethics framework.

levers.name: Cohort pacing and capacity planning for the Bonn residential unit
levers.consequences: Capacity constraints limit data yield, while safety and regulatory requirements demand predictable staffing. Effective pacing can sustain eight active suites and improve data capture, but the approach must include escalation protocols as occupancy approaches safety thresholds to maintain quality and safety.
options: Implement rolling admission with overlapping cohorts and modular staffing to sustain eight active suites while testing higher throughput without expanding footprint.
Adopt adaptive stay lengths guided by interim event yield, reallocating nights to higher-yield participants to maximize data capture.
Cross-train night-shift staff and implement on-call scheduling to flexibly cover demand and enable temporary capacity expansion without new hires.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: capacity constraints versus data yield; safety and regulatory requirements demand predictable staffing. Weakness: none of the options address explicit escalation triggers or quality-control protocols as occupancy nears safety limits.

levers.name: Sensing stack integrity and privacy-preserving data handling
levers.consequences: Enhanced data fidelity across streams improves event detection and phenotyping but increases operational complexity and privacy risk. A robust privacy framework enables external collaboration later while maintaining local control over sensitive data.
options: Standardize the tiered sensing with scheduled PSG nights and robust artifact rejection pipelines to improve event fidelity while keeping patient burden manageable.
Introduce automated real-time data quality checks and alerting to flag sensor dropout or artifact contamination before analysis.
Incorporate privacy-preserving data handling by storing de-identified streams locally with strict access controls and separate pipelines for sensitive video data.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: data richness vs privacy and burden. Weakness: options omit cross-device interoperability challenges and an auditable data governance trail across streams.

levers.name: Recruitment and retention optimization for extended stays
levers.consequences: Streamlined recruitment reduces time-to-enrollment and improves retention during multi-week stays, with downstream gains in data yield and analysis power. However, broader enrollment pathways may introduce variability in event prevalence and require careful screening to maintain data quality.
options: Formalize regional referral networks and streamlined pre-admission screening to accelerate enrollment and minimize attrition across the intake funnel.
Provide flexible scheduling, transport assistance, and enhanced on-site amenities to improve participant adherence across multi-week stays.
Engage patient advocacy and community outreach while enforcing privacy-preserving consent processes to expand awareness without compromising data governance.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: enrollment velocity versus data quality and participant burden. Weakness: options risk selection bias if criteria broaden or pre-screening becomes inconsistent across sites.

levers.name: Data governance and privacy-by-design for residential parasomnia research
levers.consequences: Clear governance reduces risk of data misuse and eases external collaboration by establishing verifiable consent and de-identification standards. Trade-offs include potential limits on data granularity and longer setup times for approvals across repositories.
options: Implement layered consent with tiered data sharing and de-identification protocols, plus data-use agreements with external repositories.
Establish a formal data access review process and role-based access controls to minimize exposure and ensure auditability.
Explore synthetic data generation and federated analysis options to enable external collaboration while safeguarding participant privacy.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: privacy guarantees reduce data utility; weakness: misses ongoing consent management and revocation as data-sharing scope evolves.

levers.name: Funding diversification and collaboration strategy
levers.consequences: A diversified funding plan mitigates single-source risk and supports core operations through milestones while enabling strategic partnerships. Trade-offs include negotiating publication terms and maintaining independence across partners.
options: Pursue milestone-based grant applications coupled with internal Bonn funding to ensure core operations stay funded through critical milestones.
Negotiate a structured pharmaceutical partnership with predefined publication windows while preserving independent interpretation of results.
Develop a multi-institution consortium for shared data standards and joint pilot grants to broaden funding opportunities.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: short-term funding milestones versus long-term independence. Weakness: lacks explicit conflict resolution and publication governance across partners.

levers.name: Governance cadence, risk thresholds, and external oversight
levers.consequences: Regular oversight aligns progress with safety and regulatory norms, enabling timely course corrections. However, excessive cadence can slow execution and create administrative load if responses lag behind findings.
options: Institute a quarterly risk dashboard with predefined thresholds and rapid escalation pathways to the advisory board.
Pilot staggered governance reviews with independent internal auditors and rotating chairs to avoid centralization and improve accountability.
Implement milestone-based budget reallocations with transparent documentation to maintain agility while preserving accountability across evolving project phases.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: speed of progress versus robust oversight. Weakness: lacks explicit remediation timelines or action plans following advisory feedback.

responses.strategic_rationale: The following levers target the Bonn residential parasomnia program’s core tensions: achieving high-resolution physiological data while minimizing participant burden; maintaining safety and ethical compliance in a live-in setting; ensuring reliable scoring and transparent data governance; and planning for scalable growth without compromising data integrity. Together they shape sensing architecture, participant experience, adjudication rigor, data sharing, operational scaling, and semi-automation, establishing a resilient path from pilot operations to a broader research platform. The levers also probe unconventional angles, such as environment-driven retention, and automated triage, to accelerate discovery without relaxing safety standards.

levers.name: Tiered sensing architecture and escalation policy for parasomnia events
levers.consequences: Adopting a tiered sensing architecture concentrates data collection on high-signal nights, improving resolution where it matters. It also lowers ongoing PSG exposure for participants, but adds hardware and maintenance complexity that could hinder rapid scale-up if escalation frequency remains high.
options: Adopt nightly low-burden monitoring across all participants, with automated flagging and scheduled escalation nights only when flagged events occur.
Pair continuous low-burden sensing with random mid-point full PSG checks to calibrate event detection without blanket full-night monitoring.
Centralize data review in a rolling two-tier system where technicians handle preliminary scoring and only escalate to PSG after cross-site consensus.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: maximizing data depth while minimizing burden and cost; Weakness: escalation protocols may over-prioritize flagged events, leaving quiet nights under-characterized and biasing prevalence estimates.

levers.name: Participant retention through stay-environment design and engagement scheduling
levers.consequences: Environment design and engagement scheduling can improve participant comfort and reduce dropout, increasing data yield over extended stays. The trade-off is a potential drift from strictly naturalistic sleep conditions and greater operational burden to manage activities and privacy controls.
options: Offer flexible stay schedules with tiered compensation tied to milestone engagement, while preserving safety and study integrity.
Implement a rotating activity calendar and partner check-ins to reduce fatigue and improve data yield during extended stays.
Introduce optional family-friendly accommodations and privacy controls to improve comfort without compromising study protocol compliance.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing participant comfort with data integrity and protocol fidelity; Weakness: enhanced engagement strategies may introduce behavioral modifications that affect sleep patterns and event rates.

levers.name: Independent adjudication workflow optimization for parasomnia events
levers.consequences: A structured adjudication workflow improves scoring consistency and study credibility. However, external or multi-site adjudication can introduce delays and higher costs, potentially slowing timely analysis.
options: Institute weekly inter-rater calibration meetings with predefined scoring rubrics and periodic blind re-annotated samples.
Implement double-blind scoring where two scorers independently annotate events, followed by adjudication meetings if disagreements exceed a threshold.
Outsource independent scoring to an external expert panel on a quarterly basis to benchmark internal scoring and reduce drift.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: achieving high inter-rater reliability while preserving timely analysis; Weakness: external benchmarking may not align with local clinical practices and adds logistical overhead.

levers.name: Data governance and sharing scope for rich physiologic datasets
levers.consequences: A robust data governance framework protects privacy and enables controlled external access. It also creates friction that may slow replication and cross-site validation if processes become too opaque or burdensome.
options: Establish a tiered data access model with role-based permissions and a data use agreement workflow for external researchers.
Tag datasets with provenance metadata and enforce an embargo period before public release to balance verification and privacy.
Set up automated de-identification pipelines and audit trails to satisfy institutional and legal data protection requirements.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: privacy protection vs data accessibility; Weakness: overly stringent controls may delay external validation and multi-site replication, reducing impact.

levers.name: Operational readiness for scalable expansion and capital planning
levers.consequences: A staged expansion plan ties capacity growth to data quality and safety milestones, enabling disciplined scaling. The trade-off is that ambitious gating criteria may slow progress if milestones prove harder to meet than anticipated.
options: Develop a staged expansion plan tied to predefined data quality and safety metrics, enabling gradual growth without overextending resources.
Pilot a modular expansion with independent subunits and shared services to test scalability while preserving core standards.
Secure contingency funding and flexible staffing contracts to absorb surges in participant enrollment without compromising safety.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: growth ambition versus quality safeguards; Weakness: gating criteria may be gamed or fail to capture unanticipated risks, delaying progress.

levers.name: Phased integration of semi-automated event-triage tools
levers.consequences: Semi-automated triage reduces manual review burden and accelerates insights, enabling faster iteration of scoring protocols. The trade-off is potential overreliance on algorithms that may misclassify ambiguous events if not properly supervised.
options: Prototype semi-automated triage on retrospective datasets, with human-in-the-loop adjudication before any live deployment.
Roll out event-triage tools across nights with an opt-in pilot per participant, evaluating sensitivity and false-positive rates before full adoption.
Develop a parallel track of algorithmic benchmarking against dual human scoring to document performance and prevent premature deployment.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: efficiency gains vs accuracy and clinician trust; Weakness: retrospective benchmarking may not reveal edge-case performance on rare parasomnia morphologies.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn parasomnia facility confronts competing demands: rigorous data collection and phenotyping amid budget, staffing, and regulatory constraints; safety and participant welfare in a live residential setting; privacy and ethics around video streams; and the imperative to demonstrate robust methods for eventual scale and grant funding. The six levers address core tensions by (1) aligning monitoring intensity with observed event yield to protect safety while conserving resources, (2) deploying tiered sensing with escalation only when warranted, (3) triggering expansion based on data-driven milestones, (4) strengthening privacy-preserving data governance for adjudication, (5) streamlining recruitment through partnerships, and (6) centralizing adjudication to improve reliability while maintaining throughput. Together these choices aim to produce credible methods validation and a path to scale.

levers.name: Nightly monitoring intensity aligned to event yield through adaptive scheduling
levers.consequences: Adjust the mix of low-burden nights versus enhanced PSG nights to reflect observed event yield, reducing unnecessary full-night studies while maintaining data depth for phenotype discovery. Trade-offs include potential delays in capturing rare episodes if escalation thresholds are overly cautious, potentially reducing study power.
options: Increase enhanced PSG intensity only after a predefined event yield threshold is met for a given cohort, then taper as yield stabilizes.
Implement rolling eligibility rules for enhanced nights based on ongoing scoring performance to preserve data quality while limiting overuse.
Schedule adaptive nights based on interim reviews by the scoring team to balance safety margins with operational efficiency.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing safety and data yield with resource constraints. Weakness: none of the options specify a rapid reallocation trigger for sudden yield shifts or participant variability.

levers.name: Tiered sensing architecture with escalation-triggered full PSG
levers.consequences: Institute a tiered model where routine nights rely on dry-electrode and contact-free sensors, with escalation to full PSG when flagged events occur; this lowers cost and participant burden while preserving diagnostic depth. Downstream: reliance on sensor thresholds risks missing atypical presentations if criteria are not well-tuned, potentially biasing event capture toward obvious episodes.
options: Deploy escalation triggers using deterministic thresholding on sensor data to automatically flag episodes for full PSG reviews.
Institute a blinded second-party review for escalation decisions to reduce false positives and protect participant privacy.
Centralize escalation decisions into a monitoring hub to reduce response times and ensure consistent protocol adherence.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: dependence on sensor thresholds to trigger escalation may miss atypical episodes. Weakness: options lack a strategy to guard against false negatives from overly conservative thresholds.

levers.name: Milestone-driven facility expansion conditional on data quality and safety metrics
levers.consequences: Expansion only proceeds after meeting predefined data quality and safety KPIs, ensuring sustainable operations; downstream, the cautious pace may slow overall research outputs and funding progress if milestones prove slow, risking delays in scale-up.
options: Proceed with physical expansion exclusively after completion of a pilot cohort demonstrating data quality targets and safe staffing ratios.
Adopt a staged lease extension with optional second-half build-out contingent on achieving publication milestones and data completeness.
Lock in flexible renovation agreements allowing rapid reconfiguration to support increased suite capacity when metrics meet acceptance criteria.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: timing expansion against proven data quality and safety; Weakness: no explicit contingency criteria if metrics are borderline or ambiguous.

levers.name: Privacy-forward video governance with redaction and controlled access
levers.consequences: Establish privacy-preserving video handling enabling adjudication without exposing identifying features; downstream, this adds governance complexity and potentially slows data sharing for external validation.
options: Implement a redaction pipeline that strips identifiable features from video streams before storage and review.
Establish role-based access controls and audit trails to limit video visibility to authorized scorers and external reviewers.
Pursue selective sharing agreements with approved researchers under strict data-use agreements to balance openness and privacy.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: privacy constraints limit data usefulness; Weakness: no explicit plan for third-party access under ethical frameworks or for differential privacy.

levers.name: Recruitment funnel optimization through regional clinician partnerships and streamlined pre-admission pathway
levers.consequences: Streamlines recruitment and improves enrollment efficiency; downstream, reliance on partner channels may bias sample composition if referral pathways skew demographics or eligibility.
options: Forge formal partnerships with regional sleep clinics to pre-screen and refer eligible participants, expediting consent and screening.
Implement centralized pre-admission checks with standardized collateral history to reduce screening variability and time-to-enrollment.
Offer transportation stipends and flexible scheduling to reduce barriers for eligible participants and maintain enrollment targets.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: fast recruitment vs representativeness; Weakness: no explicit mechanism to monitor sample diversity or manage referral bias.

levers.name: Independent adjudication workflow integration with centralized scorer arbitration
levers.consequences: Consolidates scoring for higher reliability; downstream, bottlenecks may emerge if scorer capacity is insufficient or arbitration rules become overly rigid, potentially delaying event adjudication.
options: Create a centralized adjudication hub with parallel scoring teams to balance throughput and consensus.
Implement a double-blind review protocol with predefined adjudication rules to minimize bias and accelerate decisions.
Schedule weekly calibration sessions among scorers to harmonize criteria and improve inter-rater reliability.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: reliability vs speed in adjudication; Weakness: no plan for backlog management or cross-site arbitration capacity.

Cross-train night-shift staff and implement on-call scheduling to flexibly cover demand and enable temporary capacity expansion without new hires.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions reduces expected annual loss on paper, but a single regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously, turning the diversification assumption into a concentration risk at the worst possible moment."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Length Limits**
   - Keep each `review_lever` under 2 sentences (aim for 20–40 words). Name the tension and the missed weakness concisely.
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
